title: Effective Data Visualization
summary: The principles behind charts that communicate clearly — from aesthetics mapping and color theory to modern web tooling, accessibility, and the charts people get wrong most often.

chapters.title: Why Visualization Matters
chapters.summary: Why encoding data visually accelerates comprehension, what makes a chart better than a table, and when visualization actually hurts more than it helps.

chapters.title: Mapping Data to Aesthetics
chapters.summary: How to translate data variables into visual properties — position, color, size, shape, and transparency — and why the hierarchy of those properties determines what a chart communicates.

chapters.title: Color in Data Visualization
chapters.summary: How to choose palettes that encode the right information, respect colorblind constraints, and use color to emphasize rather than decorate.

chapters.title: Visualizing Amounts
chapters.summary: When you have one number per category and want to compare them, the chart type and sort order determine whether the comparison is fast or slow — and whether the bars mislead or inform.

chapters.title: Visualizing Distributions
chapters.summary: Showing how data is spread across a range — histograms, density plots, violin plots, box plots, and ridgeline plots — and knowing when each reveals something the others hide.

chapters.title: Visualizing Proportions and Part-of-Whole
chapters.summary: When the question is "what fraction of the whole does each part represent?" — pie charts, stacked bars, treemaps, and waffle charts each answer it with different tradeoffs.

chapters.title: Visualizing Relationships
chapters.summary: Scatterplots, line charts, bubble charts, and connected scatterplots — how to show the relationship between two or more variables without implying false causation or obscuring real correlation.

chapters.title: Visualizing Many Variables
chapters.summary: When a dataset has more than two meaningful variables, pairs plots, parallel coordinates, heatmaps, and dimensionality reduction let you see the multivariate structure without reducing everything to a single number.

chapters.title: Geography, Uncertainty, and Overlapping Data
chapters.summary: How to encode geographic data without letting projection choice mislead, how to represent uncertainty honestly rather than hiding it, and how to handle overplotted data without losing the signal.

chapters.title: Time Series and Trends
chapters.summary: Line charts, smoothing, seasonality, and common mistakes in trend visualization — how to show what's happening over time without creating false impressions of momentum or change.

chapters.title: Annotation, Titles, and Clutter Removal
chapters.summary: The non-data elements that make or break a chart — how to write titles that interpret rather than describe, use annotations to guide attention, and strip clutter without stripping meaning.

chapters.title: Misleading Visualizations
chapters.summary: The most common ways charts lie — truncated axes, dual scales, area illusions, cherry-picked ranges, and 3D distortions — and how to identify them in charts you're reading as well as ones you're building.

chapters.title: Modern Tools and Accessibility
chapters.summary: The 2026 visualization tooling landscape — Observable Plot, Vega-Lite, D3, deck.gl, ggplot2, and notebook-native reactive charts — plus what accessibility actually requires from the charts you build.
